content.full_text:
State of California
HEALTH AND WELFARE AGENCY
1600 NINTH STREET, ROOM 460
SACRAMENTO 95814

PETE WILSON
GOVERNOR

April 22, 1992

Mr. Scot Hallabrin, Esq.
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I am an Assistant Secretary at the California Health and Welfare
Agency and have a legal responsibility to file a statement of my
economic interests with my employer. I am writing to request your
guidance regarding the possible impacts my wife's employment may
have on my statement of economic interests and the scope of my
duties as Assistant Secretary.

There are five Assistant Secretaries at the Health and Welfare
Agency, all of whom report to the Deputy Secretary for Program and
Fiscal Affairs, who in turn reports to the Undersecretary and
Secretary of the Agency. Each of the Assistant Secretaries has
been given oversight responsibility for a portion of the eleven
health and welfare departments which comprise the Health and
Welfare Agency. I have the public health assignment. In this
assignment, I oversee the public health programs administered by
the Department of Health Services (DHS) as well as the Office of
Statewide Health Planning and Development (OSHPD) and the Emergency
Medical Services Authority (EMSA). My role is to act in a staff
capacity to the Deputy Secretary, and in turn the Undersecretary
and Secretary of the Agency, by reviewing and analyzing the program
and fiscal policies of these departments and making recommendations
regarding the impacts of these policies.

Within the scope of the public health assignment is a DHS program
which concerns the construction and operation of a low-level
radioactive waste (LLRW) disposal facility in San Bernardino County
(Ward Valley). The purpose of the waste facility is for the
disposal of LLRW from California's academic, industrial,
pharmaceutical, utility and biotechnology sectors. Federal law
requires states to have constructed operating facilities by January
1, 1993, or be subject to certain penalties. It is with respect to
this DHS program that I am writing to request your guidance.

My wife is a Research Associate with the Davis, California,
biotechnology firm, Calgene, Incorporated. She holds a 60% time
salaried position which pays approximately $22,000 annually. In
addition, during the past six months she has cashed stock options

FFPC

APR 24 12 25 PM [TELEPHONE
(916) 654-3454
FAX
(916) 654-3343

Mr. Scot Hallabrin, Esq.
Page 2

totalling approximately $4,000 and holds a stock option which
matures in June, 1992, which is worth approximately $5,000.

As a part of its research and development activities, Calgene uses
radioactive materials. As a result, Calgene is a generator of
radioactive waste. Currently, like other waste generators in
California, Calgene ships its waste to either Nevada or Washington,
which have disposal facilities. It is my understanding that
Calgene's current cost of shipping its waste out of state is
approximately $12,000 to $15,000 per year. I have not formally
verified this figure.

Under California statute, the costs associated with the proposed
LLRW disposal facility at Ward Valley, which is under DHS licensing
and monitoring, are to be paid by waste generators through an
increase in waste disposal fees. The projected cost for disposal
at the Ward Valley facility is approximately $325 to $350 per cubic
foot. The current cost for waste disposal at out-of-state
facilities is approximately $75 per cubic foot. However, this cost
is expected to increase significantly on January 1, 1993, at which
time federal law authorizes states with existing facilities to no
longer accept waste from outside their compacts (regional areas).
Currently, states with disposal facilities must accept waste from
all waste generators, regardless of state or origin, as long as the
waste generator pays the cost of disposal.

While federal law requires states to have operating facilities by
January 1, 1993, the earliest date an operating facility is
anticipated in California is January 1, 1994. As a result, current
waste disposal costs (@ $75/cf) are expected to increase
significantly on January 1, 1993, either because states with
facilities (which can limit access) will increase disposal costs to
waste generators (in states which do not have facilities) or
because states with facilities will deny access altogether to
states outside their regional areas (such as California) and waste
generators will be forced to take other, more expensive steps to
address their waste disposal.

Calgene, Inc., is a corporation whose stock is traded publicly on
the NASDAC. It is my understanding that when a company's stock is
traded on the NASDAC, a public official has a conflict of interest
if he or she derives income from that company and is involved in a
decision which has a cost or savings to the company in the amount
of $50,000 or more. In light of my wife's employment with Calgene,
I need your guidance on the following questions:

1)    How should I report my wife's annual income, cashed stock
      options and uncashed stock options?

Mr. Scot Hallabrin, Esq.
Page 3

2)     Under what conditions, if any, would I be permitted to serve
the Health and Welfare Agency with respect to the proposed
LLRW disposal facility in Ward Valley?

3)     With respect to the regulation which specifies a conflict of
interest involving decisions which have a cost or savings to
a company in the amount of $50,000 or more, how would this
figure be calculated in this particular situation?     Would
current costs of disposal be the basis for an analysis or
would costs effective January 1, 1993, be the basis for an
analysis?

4)     Please explain the $50,000 limitation which is specified for
NASDAC-traded companies.     Are there distinctions between a
company which is directly involved with the decision (e.g. a
contractor who stands to gain a contract because of the
decision) and a company which is indirectly involved in the
decision (e.g. a company which is affected by the impact of
the decision)?

5)     If my situation precludes me from involvement in an analytical
capacity regarding the proposed LLRW disposal facility, does
it also preclude me from speaking to the press and the public
regarding the proposed Ward Valley facility in a public
affairs capacity?

Thank you very much for your time and consideration of my request.
I look forward to hearing from you.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Lee D. Kemper
Assistant Secretary
Program and Fiscal Affairs

cc:     S. Kimberly Belshe'
Deputy Secretary, Health and Welfare Agency

sections.question: 1) How should I report my wife's annual income, cashed stock options and uncashed stock options? 2) Under what conditions, if any, would I be permitted to serve the Health and Welfare Agency with respect to the proposed LLRW disposal facility in Ward Valley? 3) With respect to the regulation which specifies a conflict of interest involving decisions which have a cost or savings to a company in the amount of $50,000 or more, how would this figure be calculated in this particular situation? Would current costs of disposal be the basis for an analysis or would costs effective January 1, 1993, be the basis for an analysis? 4) Please explain the $50,000 limitation which is specified for NASDAQ-traded companies. Are there distinctions between a company which is directly involved with the decision (e.g. a contractor who stands to gain a contract because of the decision) and a company which is indirectly involved in the decision (e.g. a company which is affected by the impact of the decision)? 5) If my situation precludes me from involvement in an analytical capacity regarding the proposed LLRW disposal facility, does it also preclude me from speaking to the press and the public regarding the proposed Ward Valley facility in a public affairs capacity?
sections.question_synthetic: An Assistant Secretary at the California Health and Welfare Agency seeks guidance on conflict of interest requirements regarding his wife's employment with Calgene, a biotechnology company that generates radioactive waste, in relation to his oversight of the proposed low-level radioactive waste disposal facility at Ward Valley. He requests clarification on proper financial reporting and the conditions under which he may participate in decisions affecting the facility.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. The document contains five specific numbered questions but no corresponding conclusion or answer section from FPPC. The document is dated April 22, 1992 (year appears as '799?' with unclear digit, but context indicates 1992 from document header). Minor OCR issues present but do not significantly impact extraction.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: 1) How should I report my wife's annual income, cashed stock options and uncashed stock options? 2) Under what conditions, if any, would I be permitted to serve the Health and Welfare Agency with respect to the proposed LLRW disposal facility in Ward Valley? 3) With respect to the regulation which specifies a conflict of interest involving decisions which have a cost or savings to a company in the amount of $50,000 or more, how would this figure be calculated in this particular situation? Would current costs of disposal be the basis for an analysis or would costs effective January 1, 1993, be the basis for an analysis? 4) Please explain the $50,000 limitation which is specified for NASDAQ-traded companies. Are there distinctions between a company which is directly involved with the decision (e.g. a contractor who stands to gain a contract because of the decision) and a company which is indirectly involved in the decision (e.g. a company which is affected by the impact of the decision)? 5) If my situation precludes me from involvement in an analytical capacity regarding the proposed LLRW disposal facility, does it also preclude me from speaking to the press and the public regarding the proposed Ward Valley facility in a public affairs capacity?

embedding.summary: An incoming request from an Assistant Secretary at the California Health and Welfare Agency seeking FPPC guidance on potential conflicts of interest arising from his wife's employment with Calgene, Inc., a radioactive waste generator, in connection with his oversight duties regarding the proposed Ward Valley LLRW disposal facility.
